items.q: What does a gene code for?
items.correct: Typically a polypeptide (protein) — through transcription to mRNA and translation by ribosomes.
items.misconception: A gene codes for a specific physical feature like eye colour directly.
items.random_wrong: Each gene codes for an organ or body part as a whole.
items.why: Students learn 'gene for eye colour' shorthand, then think genes directly produce traits rather than producing proteins that contribute to traits.

items.q: What does it mean for an allele to be 'dominant'?
items.correct: It is expressed in the phenotype when at least one copy is present (in a heterozygote).
items.misconception: A dominant allele is the most common allele in a population.
items.random_wrong: A dominant allele physically destroys the recessive allele in the genome.
items.why: The everyday meaning of 'dominant' (most prevalent, strongest) misleads students into thinking it relates to frequency or power.

items.correct: No — humans and chimpanzees share a common ancestor; both have evolved from it.
items.misconception: Humans evolved directly from chimpanzees, which is why chimps still exist as a less-evolved version.
items.random_wrong: Humans evolved from monkeys but chimps evolved from gorillas.
items.why: The phrase 'descended from apes' and linear evolution diagrams encourage 'A turned into B' thinking, missing the branching tree model.

items.q: Did humans evolve directly from chimpanzees?
items.correct: No — humans and chimpanzees share a common ancestor; both have evolved separately from it.
items.misconception: Humans evolved directly from chimpanzees, which is why chimps still exist.
items.random_wrong: Humans evolved from monkeys while chimps evolved from gorillas.
items.why: Students read evolution as a ladder, but it is a branching tree; living relatives are cousins descended from a shared ancestor, not our ancestors.

items.q: Do organisms evolve features on purpose because they need them?
items.correct: No — useful features arise from random heritable variation; those that aid survival become more common through natural selection.
items.misconception: Organisms grow the features they need by choosing to adapt.
items.random_wrong: The environment inserts new genes into organisms that need them.
items.why: Students see evolution as goal-directed, but variation is random and undirected; selection, not intention, makes helpful traits spread.

items.q: How many pairs of chromosomes does a human body cell have?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 23. “13” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: A section of DNA that codes for a protein
items.why: Correct answer: A section of DNA that codes for a protein. “A whole chromosome” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If a human body cell has 46 chromosomes, how many chromosomes does a human sperm or egg cell carry?
items.why: The real figure is 23. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
